macro_segments.title: The Sanctity of Shemitah (Sabbatical Year) and What Produce Qualifies

Rava's Resolution: Default Use of Wood Determines Shemitah Status

macro_segments.title: The Tannaic Debate on Expanding "Le'Achla" (For Eating) and Common Use

micro_segments.title: Tannaitic Sources: Wine Use for Soaking Flax and Laundering
Rabbi Yehuda's Expansion: Things Common to All People (Shaveh L'Chol Nefesh)
The Braitah on Poultices, Heating, and Other Excluded Uses
Connection to Firewood: Which Tannaim Apply to Wood's Shemitah Status?

macro_segments.title: The Mishna: Dyeing Wool with Wrong Color—Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yehuda

micro_segments.title: The Case: White Wool Dyed Black (or Vice Versa) and Payment Obligations
Rabbi Meir's Position: Shinuy Koneh (Change in Possession)
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Lesser of Expenses or Benefit Increase

macro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Ruling and Rav Yosef's Objection to the Precedent

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna: We Rule Like Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha and Rabbi Yehuda
Rav Yosef's Challenge: Why Rule Like a Minority Opinion?
Explanation of Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha on Loans Before Holidays

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Anonymous Mishna Superseding Dispute (Stam Achar Machlokes)

micro_segments.title: The Rule: Anonymous Mishna That Follows a Debate Determines Halacha
Rav Yosef's Counterargument: Order Matters Within One Masechet
Rav Huna's Response: Nezikin as a Unified Masechet Despite Division
Alternative Justification: Categorical Statements (Kol Hamish'neh) Override

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Shaliach Who Bought Wrong Grain (Barley Instead of Wheat)

micro_segments.title: Two Braitot: Ownership and Profit-Loss Distribution When Instructions Violated
First Braitah: Complete Ownership Transfers to Shaliach; He Bears All Loss/Gain
Second Braitah: Ba'al Habayis Retains Ownership; Splits Profit When Loss Occurs
Concern: How Second Braitah Could Create Workaround for Charging Interest (Ribbis)

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Analysis: Debate Between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yehuda

micro_segments.title: First Braitah Reflects Rabbi Meir; Second Reflects Rabbi Yehuda
Rabbi Meir: Shinuy (Violation of Instructions) Means Ownership Transfers
Rabbi Yehuda: Shinuy Does Not Transfer Ownership, Profit-Loss Splits Continue

macro_segments.title: Rav Elazar's Refinement: The Distinction Between Personal Use and Commercial Product

micro_segments.title: Challenge: Does Rabbi Meir's Shinuy Apply Only to Personal Use Items?
Rav Elazar's Proposal: Both Cases Go by Rabbi Meir but Context Differs

macro_segments.title: The Question of Agent Representation: Does Seller Need to Know?

micro_segments.title: Challenge: How Does Ba'al Habayis Acquire When Instructions Are Violated?
Rav Shmuel bar Sasrati's Objection: Even Perfect Compliance Requires Knowledge
Rabbi Abahu's Response: Example of Wife's Clothing and the Principle of Zachin

macro_segments.title: Extended Debate on Whether Third Party Must Know the Agent

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Abba's Objection: Wife's Clothing Case Does Not Prove Third-Party Knowledge Unnecessary
Rabbi Zeira Challenges: The Tefillin Case Shows Intent Irrelevant
Abaye Resists: Tefillin and Wife's Items Are Distinct Cases
Rav Oshaya's Counter: The Seizing of Collateral (Arachin) Proves Third-Party Knowledge Irrelevant
Rabbi Abba's Final Response: Hekdesh Cases Are Exceptional; General Principle Unproven

macro_segments.title: Unresolved Question: Can Shaliach Acquire for Ba'al Habayis When Violating Instructions Without Third-Party Knowledge?

micro_segments.title: Summary: The Logical Problem Remains When Shaliach Acts Against Orders
Proposed Solution: Assume Seller Always Knows Shaliach Represents Someone
Closure: If Seller Knows and Intends Transfer, Violation Is Irrelevant to Acquisition
